walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Boards
walkthrough.text: Let's get Tiny Towns set up. First, give each player one four-by-four grid board. This is your town, and every space on the grid will eventually be filled with resources or buildings. Place your board in front of you with enough room around it for resource cubes.

walkthrough.title: Select the Building Cards
walkthrough.text: Now let's choose which buildings will be available in this game. There are seven building types, and each type has multiple card options for variety. For your first game, use the recommended starter set shown in the rulebook. For future games, you can pick one card per building type to customize the game. Place all seven selected building cards face up where everyone can see them.

walkthrough.title: Deal Monument Cards
walkthrough.text: Monuments are unique buildings with powerful scoring abilities. Look at both cards secretly, choose one to keep, and return the other face down without revealing it. Keep your monument card hidden until you build it during the game.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Resource Supply
walkthrough.text: Place all five types of resource cubes in shared piles in the center of the table. The five resource types are wood, which is brown. Wheat, which is yellow. Brick, which is red. Glass, which is blue. And stone, which is gray. Make sure everyone can easily reach all five piles.

walkthrough.title: Set Out Building Miniatures
walkthrough.text: Place all the small wooden building miniatures in a shared supply near the resource cubes. Each building type has its own color and shape. When you construct a building during the game, you will swap resource cubes off your grid and place one of these miniatures in their spot.

walkthrough.title: Choose the Starting Master Builder
walkthrough.text: Pick a starting player and give them the Master Builder hammer token. The Master Builder has a special role each turn. They name a resource type, and every player must place that resource on their grid. The hammer passes clockwise after each turn. Everyone is ready to start building their tiny town.

summary.title: Choose the Starting Master Builder

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Tiny Towns is a pattern-building puzzle game for one to six players. Each turn, the Master Builder names a resource, and every player must place one cube of that resource on their four-by-four grid. When cubes on your grid match the pattern on a building card, you can construct that building. Your goal is to fill your grid with high-scoring buildings and avoid empty spaces, which cost you points at the end of the game.

walkthrough.title: The Master Builder's Role
walkthrough.text: The player with the hammer token is the Master Builder. At the start of each turn, the Master Builder names one of the five resource types. Then every player at the table, including the Master Builder, must take one cube of that resource and place it on any empty space on their own grid. The Master Builder gets to choose strategically. They can name resources that help themselves while potentially giving opponents resources they do not want.

walkthrough.title: Placing Resources
walkthrough.text: When you place a resource cube, it goes on any empty space on your four-by-four grid. You cannot stack cubes, and you cannot place on a space already occupied by a building. Placement is mandatory. Even if the named resource does not help you, you must still place it somewhere. This is the core tension of the game. Every cube you place takes up precious space on your tiny grid.

walkthrough.title: Constructing Buildings
walkthrough.text: After placing your resource cube, you may construct one or more buildings. To construct, the resource cubes on your grid must match the exact pattern shown on a building card. The pattern can be rotated ninety, one hundred eighty, or two hundred seventy degrees, but it cannot be mirrored. Remove all the resource cubes used in the pattern and place the corresponding building miniature on any one of the spaces those cubes occupied. You choose which space gets the building.

walkthrough.title: Pattern Matching Details
walkthrough.text: Let's be precise about patterns. The building card shows which resources go where relative to each other. The resources must be in the correct positions on your grid, but the entire pattern can be anywhere on the board and can be rotated. You cannot flip or mirror the pattern. If a building requires a two-by-two square of resources, those four cubes must form an actual two-by-two square on your grid, in the correct colors and positions.

walkthrough.title: Multiple Builds Per Turn
walkthrough.text: If placing one resource cube completes multiple building patterns on your grid, you may construct all of them in a single turn. You can also build the same building type multiple times if you have multiple completed patterns. There is no limit to how many buildings you can construct in one turn. Just make sure each pattern is valid before removing the cubes.

walkthrough.title: The Factory Building
walkthrough.text: One special building type deserves extra attention. The Factory, once constructed on your grid, lets you place an additional resource of your choice on each turn, after the Master Builder's resource. This means you effectively get two resource placements per turn. Multiple Factories do not stack. Even with two Factories, you only get one extra resource per turn. Building a Factory early is a significant advantage.

walkthrough.title: Monuments
walkthrough.text: Your monument is a unique secret building that only you can construct. It has its own resource pattern and scoring condition, just like the shared buildings. Keep it hidden until you build it, then reveal the card. You can only build your monument once during the game. Monuments often have powerful scoring conditions that can be worth many points if built at the right time.

walkthrough.title: Grid Full and Game End
walkthrough.text: The game ends for each player individually when their four-by-four grid is completely full. All sixteen spaces must be occupied by either resource cubes or buildings. Once your grid is full, you stop participating in turns. Other players continue until their grids are also full. The game officially ends when every player's grid is complete.

walkthrough.title: Scoring
walkthrough.text: When the game ends, score each building on your grid according to its card's rules. Cottages score if they are fed by an adjacent farm building. Taverns score based on set collection. Theaters score for unique building types in the same row and column. Each building type has its own scoring method. After totaling building points, subtract one point for each empty space on your grid. The player with the most points wins. If tied, the player with fewer empty spaces wins.

Match patterns → construct buildings → score VP
Empty spaces at end = -1 VP each

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your Tiny Town
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through several turns with four players to demonstrate how resource placement, pattern building, and construction work. Follow along even if you have a different player count at your table.

walkthrough.title: Starting Conditions
walkthrough.text: Everyone has an empty four-by-four grid, sixteen open spaces. The seven building cards are displayed for all to see. Each player has a secret monument card. Player one has the Master Builder hammer. Let's begin building our tiny towns.

walkthrough.title: Turn One: Master Builder Names Wood
walkthrough.text: Everyone takes a brown wood cube and places it on their grid. Player one places wood in the bottom-left corner, starting a planned pattern. Player two places in the center. Player three places in the top-right corner. Player four places in the bottom row. Each player has a different strategy in mind.

walkthrough.title: Turn Two: Master Builder Names Brick
walkthrough.text: Everyone places a red brick cube. Player one puts brick next to their wood, forming two-thirds of a Cottage pattern. Player three puts brick adjacent to their wood, also starting a pattern. Notice how every player is working on different building patterns simultaneously.

walkthrough.title: Turn Three: Completing a Pattern
walkthrough.text: Player three names wheat. Player one places wheat to complete the Cottage pattern on their grid. The three cubes now match the Cottage card exactly. Player one removes all three resource cubes and places a blue Cottage miniature on one of the three freed spaces. Player one has constructed their first building.

walkthrough.title: Why Early Cottages Are Great
walkthrough.text: Player one's early Cottage is a smart play. Cottages are a reliable point source, and building them early frees up grid space for future resources. The key is that Player one planned ahead, placing each resource knowing exactly where the pattern was heading. Planning two or three moves ahead is essential in Tiny Towns.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Random Placement
walkthrough.text: Player four has been placing resources wherever they feel convenient without a plan. After four turns, their grid has scattered cubes that do not form any pattern. Those cubes are now stuck, taking up space without contributing to any building. Worse, future resources from the Master Builder might not match what Player four needs, making the situation even harder.

walkthrough.title: Recovery: Start Planning Now
walkthrough.text: Player four, it is not too late. Look at the building cards and find a pattern that uses the resources already on your grid. Even if only one or two cubes align, you can place future resources to complete that pattern. From now on, before placing any cube, decide which building pattern you are working toward. A simple mental plan for two or three turns ahead makes a huge difference.

walkthrough.title: The Factory Advantage
walkthrough.text: Player two has been strategically working toward the Factory pattern. They complete it and place a Factory on their grid. Now, every turn after the Master Builder names a resource, Player two gets to place a second resource of their own choice. This is a massive advantage. Player two effectively gets twice the control over their grid compared to everyone else.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Ignoring Grid Space
walkthrough.text: Player four is running into trouble. Their scattered resources plus a few buildings have filled up twelve of sixteen spaces, and they still have uncompleted patterns clogging the grid. Those orphaned cubes cannot form any building and are essentially dead weight. Each one will cost a negative point at game end.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Space is Your Most Precious Resource
walkthrough.text: The lesson is that your sixteen grid spaces are more valuable than any single building. Every cube placed without a plan is a potential dead space. Before placing a resource, always ask yourself: which building am I working toward, and where exactly will the remaining pieces of this pattern go? If you cannot answer that question, you are gambling with your limited space.

walkthrough.title: Master Builder Strategy
walkthrough.text: Player two, now comfortable with their Factory advantage, names glass as Master Builder. They need glass for their next pattern, but they also noticed Player one needs wheat to complete a Tavern. By naming glass instead, Player two helps themselves while denying Player one the resource they wanted. This is the strategic heart of the Master Builder role.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Ignoring Opponents
walkthrough.text: Player three has been naming resources purely based on their own needs without looking at other players' boards. They accidentally name the exact resource Player one needs to complete a high-scoring Theater. As Master Builder, you should glance at opponents' boards before naming a resource. Help yourself, but avoid handing opponents exactly what they need.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Be a Strategic Master Builder
walkthrough.text: When you are the Master Builder, take five seconds to look at your opponents' boards before naming a resource. If two resources both help you equally, choose the one that helps your opponents least. You do not need to be mean about it, but being thoughtful with your resource naming is a significant competitive advantage.

walkthrough.title: Continue Building
walkthrough.text: Play continues with the hammer rotating clockwise each turn. As your grid fills up, decisions get harder. Every placement matters more because there is less room for error. Keep working toward building patterns, construct as soon as patterns are ready, and always think about where your next two or three resources will go.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen how resource placement works, watched buildings get constructed from patterns, witnessed the Factory advantage, and learned from three beginner mistakes. Remember to always plan ahead, treat space as your most precious resource, and use the Master Builder role strategically. You are ready to build your own tiny town.

walkthrough.title: Before You Build
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for pattern planning and grid management strategies.

Second, reference the Appendix for building scoring rules and resource details.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Plan Before You Place
walkthrough.text: The single most important skill in Tiny Towns is planning ahead. Before placing any resource cube, decide which building pattern you are working toward and exactly where each remaining piece of that pattern will go. Visualize the completed pattern on your grid. If the Master Builder names a resource you do not need, have a backup plan for where to put it without ruining your primary pattern. Two or three turns of foresight separates winners from losers.

walkthrough.title: Build the Factory Early
walkthrough.text: The Factory is the strongest building you can construct because it gives you an extra resource placement every turn. Getting a Factory built in the first five or six turns effectively doubles your control over your grid for the rest of the game. Prioritize the Factory pattern early, even if it means delaying other buildings. The compounding benefit of extra placements over many turns far outweighs a few early points from a different building.

walkthrough.title: Work from Edges and Corners
walkthrough.text: Start your building patterns from the edges and corners of your grid rather than the center. Patterns built in corners use less flexible space, leaving the more versatile central spaces open for future patterns. If you build in the center first, you create isolated pockets around the edges that are difficult to fill efficiently. Corner-first building keeps your options open.

walkthrough.title: Know Your Building Scoring
walkthrough.text: Each building type scores differently, and understanding these scoring rules is crucial. Cottages score only if fed by an adjacent farm-type building. Taverns use set collection scoring where more taverns equals exponentially more points. Theaters score based on unique building types in their row and column. Read each building card carefully at the start of the game and plan which combinations work best together.

walkthrough.title: Avoid Dead Cubes
walkthrough.text: A dead cube is a resource on your grid that cannot be part of any building pattern. It takes up one of your sixteen precious spaces and costs you one point at game end. The moment you realize a cube is stranded, consider building a low-value building that uses it rather than leaving it as dead weight. Even a two-point building is better than a negative one-point empty space, turning a three-point swing in your favor.

walkthrough.title: Use the Master Builder Strategically
walkthrough.text: When you are the Master Builder, look at your opponents' boards before naming a resource. Choose resources that advance your own patterns while being unhelpful to opponents. If you need wheat and glass equally, but your opponent desperately needs wheat, name glass instead. The Master Builder power is not just about helping yourself. It is about controlling the pace of the game.

walkthrough.title: Monument Timing
walkthrough.text: Your monument is a unique building with powerful scoring, but timing matters. Some monuments score more when built early because they interact with buildings constructed after them. Others score based on conditions at game end, so they benefit from a late build. Read your monument's scoring condition carefully and decide when the optimal construction timing is. Building a late-game monument that rewards many adjacent buildings only works if you have space planned for it.

walkthrough.title: Accept Imperfection
walkthrough.text: Not every game will go perfectly. Sometimes the Master Builder names resources you cannot use, and dead cubes accumulate. When this happens, accept the situation and pivot. Build simpler buildings to clear space rather than chasing an elaborate pattern that will never come together. Flexibility and damage control are as important as having a great plan. The player who adapts best usually wins.

walkthrough.title: The Cottage-Farm Engine
walkthrough.text: One of the most reliable strategies is building cottages fed by a central farm-type building. Place a farm-type building in a position adjacent to multiple cottage slots. Each fed cottage scores its full value, and a cluster of three or four cottages around one farm can generate twelve or more points from a compact area. Plan the farm position first, then build cottages around it.

walkthrough.title: Tavern Set Collection
walkthrough.text: Taverns scale exponentially. One tavern scores two points, but five taverns score twenty points. If you commit to the tavern strategy, aim for at least four taverns to hit the fourteen-point threshold. The tavern pattern is usually simple, so you can build them quickly. Pair this strategy with a Factory for extra resource control, and you can fill your grid with taverns efficiently.

walkthrough.title: Theater Placement Optimization
walkthrough.text: Theaters score based on unique building types in their row and column. To maximize a Theater's value, place it at the intersection of a row and column that will contain many different building types. A Theater in a row with three other unique building types and a column with two more can score five or six points. Plan your grid layout to concentrate variety around theater positions.

walkthrough.title: Factory Rush Strategy
walkthrough.text: The Factory rush dedicates your first three or four turns entirely to building the Factory. Once constructed, you have a massive advantage for the rest of the game. Use your extra resource each turn to build patterns faster than opponents. The risk is that you spend early turns not scoring, but the compound benefit of fifteen or more extra resource placements over the game's duration usually makes up for the slow start.

walkthrough.title: Monument Synergy
walkthrough.text: Your monument's scoring condition should drive your overall strategy. If your monument rewards adjacent cottages, build a cottage cluster strategy. If it rewards being surrounded by different building types, plan a diverse grid. The strongest games happen when your monument synergizes with two or three of the shared building types. Evaluate this synergy during setup when choosing which monument to keep.

walkthrough.title: Spatial Efficiency
walkthrough.text: Advanced players think about spatial efficiency. Every building pattern removes multiple cubes but only leaves one building behind. A three-cube pattern frees up two extra spaces. A four-cube pattern frees three. Use this freed space immediately for the next pattern. Chain your constructions so that the space freed by one building is immediately used to start the next pattern. Overlapping planned patterns on the same grid spaces creates maximum efficiency.

walkthrough.title: Denial as Master Builder
walkthrough.text: When you are comfortable with your own grid, shift focus to denial. Name resources that create dead cubes for opponents. If an opponent is one wheat away from completing a building, name anything except wheat. If two opponents need different resources, name the one that helps neither. This is especially powerful in the late game when opponents have less grid space to absorb unwanted cubes.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Grid Management
walkthrough.text: In the final turns, every empty space on your grid becomes critical. The penalty is only one point per empty space, but the difference between a building worth three points and an empty space worth negative one is a four-point swing. In the last few turns, prioritize completing any pattern possible, even low-value buildings. A full grid of mediocre buildings scores far better than a half-empty grid with a few high-value showpieces.

bullets: Place farm-type building adjacent to multiple cottage slots
3-4 fed cottages around 1 farm = 12+ points from compact area
Plan farm position first, build cottages around it

summary.title: Tavern Set Collection
bullets: 1/2/3/4/5 taverns = 2/5/9/14/20 VP (exponential scaling)
Aim for 4+ taverns for 14+ VP
Simple pattern + Factory = fast tavern production

summary.title: Theater Placement Optimization

Plan grid layout to concentrate variety around theaters

entries.definition: The player holding the hammer token. Names one resource type each turn that all players must place. Hammer passes clockwise.
Small colored cubes representing five resource types. Placed on grid spaces and removed when constructing buildings.
Brown resource cube. One of five resource types used in building patterns.
Yellow resource cube. One of five resource types used in building patterns.
Red resource cube. One of five resource types used in building patterns.
Blue resource cube. One of five resource types used in building patterns.
Gray resource cube. One of five resource types used in building patterns.
Building type that scores its printed value only if fed. Feeding requires adjacency to a completed farm-type building. Typically scores three VP when fed.
Building type that scores based on the number of fed cottages on your grid at the time of construction. Build after establishing cottages for maximum value.
Building type using set collection scoring. 1 tavern = 2 VP, 2 = 5, 3 = 9, 4 = 14, 5 = 20. Exponential scaling rewards committing to multiple taverns.
Building type that scores based on the number of unique building types in the same row and column as the Theater. Place at intersections of diverse rows and columns.
Building that grants one extra resource placement of your choice each turn. Extra resource placed after the Master Builder's resource. Multiple Factories do not stack.
A unique secret building dealt to each player. Has its own resource pattern and scoring condition. Can only be built once per game. Revealed when constructed.
Resources on your grid must match a building card's pattern exactly. Patterns can be rotated 90/180/270 degrees but cannot be mirrored or flipped.
When resources match a building pattern, remove all resource cubes used and place the building miniature on any one of the freed spaces.
A resource cube stranded on the grid that cannot be part of any building pattern. Effectively wastes a grid space and costs one VP as an empty space penalty at game end.
Each grid space not occupied by a building at game end costs minus one victory point. Filling your entire grid is ideal.
One of sixteen spaces on the four-by-four player board. Can hold one resource cube or one building miniature. Never more than one item per space.
Card showing a building's resource pattern and scoring rules. Seven building types displayed face up for all players during the game.
A cottage that is adjacent to a completed farm-type building. Only fed cottages score their printed value. Unfed cottages score zero.
Single-player variant where you draw from a shuffled resource card deck instead of using a Master Builder. Choose one of three face-up resource cards each turn.
Alternative mode where all players choose from available resources instead of having a Master Builder dictate. Adds more individual control.
